## Results

We screened public human RNA-seq libraries for papillomavirus reads under three discovery strategies (Methods): an unbiased prevalence sweep of skin and related tissues (`r comma(stats$n_screened)` libraries screened), a targeted query of productive cutaneous lesions providing positive controls (`r stats$n_targeted` wart/epidermodysplasia-verruciformis skin libraries), and a broadened skin-neoplasia sweep capturing cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC), actinic keratosis (AK) and Bowen disease (`r comma(stats$n_csc)` libraries screened). Throughout, every screened library, including HPV-negative ones, was scored as papillomavirus reads per million (RPM) so that infection burden could be separated from sequencing depth, and each sample was annotated as primary tissue, cell line, or engineered material.

### HPV signal in public RNA-seq is dominated by cell-line and engineered artefacts

In the unbiased sweep, HPV positivity was strongly concentrated in non-primary material. Engineered libraries were HPV-positive in `r f2(stats$eng_pct)`% of cases (95% CI `r pci(stats$eng_lo, stats$eng_hi)`) and cell-line libraries in `r f2(stats$cl_pct)`% (`r pci(stats$cl_lo, stats$cl_hi)`), versus only `r f2(stats$clin_pct)`% (`r pci(stats$clin_lo, stats$clin_hi)`) of clinical primary-tissue libraries (@tbl-positivity). In logistic regression this corresponded to roughly four- to five-fold higher odds of positivity for engineered (OR `r fo(stats$eng_or)`, 95% CI `r oci(stats$eng_or_lo, stats$eng_or_hi)`, *P* = `r fp(stats$eng_or_p)`) and cell-line (OR `r fo(stats$cl_or)`, `r oci(stats$cl_or_lo, stats$cl_or_hi)`, *P* = `r fp(stats$cl_or_p)`) samples relative to clinical tissue (likelihood-ratio *P* = `r fp(stats$lr_p)`). These positives were almost entirely the high-risk mucosal alpha types: HPV18 accounted for `r stats$hpv18_cl`/`r stats$hpv18_tot` of its calls in cell lines and HPV16 for `r stats$hpv16_cl`/`r stats$hpv16_tot` (@supptbl-types), the expected signature of integrated-HPV cell lines such as HeLa and of their well-documented cross-contamination of archived sequencing data. Genuine primary skin therefore carries HPV at a low rate (2–4%, below), and the apparent abundance of HPV in public transcriptomes is largely a laboratory artefact that must be partitioned out before any prevalence claim.

| Sample class | Libraries (*n*) | HPV+ (*n*) | HPV+ % (95% CI) | Odds ratio (95% CI) | *P* |
|:---|---:|---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Clinical (primary tissue) | `r comma(stats$clin_n)` | `r stats$clin_pos` | `r f2(stats$clin_pct)` (`r pci(stats$clin_lo, stats$clin_hi)`) | 1 (reference) | n/a |
| Engineered | `r comma(stats$eng_n)` | `r stats$eng_pos` | `r f2(stats$eng_pct)` (`r pci(stats$eng_lo, stats$eng_hi)`) | `r fo(stats$eng_or)` (`r oci(stats$eng_or_lo, stats$eng_or_hi)`) | `r fp(stats$eng_or_p)` |
| Cell line | `r comma(stats$cl_n)` | `r stats$cl_pos` | `r f2(stats$cl_pct)` (`r pci(stats$cl_lo, stats$cl_hi)`) | `r fo(stats$cl_or)` (`r oci(stats$cl_or_lo, stats$cl_or_hi)`) | `r fp(stats$cl_or_p)` |

: HPV positivity in public RNA-seq is concentrated in cell-line and engineered libraries, not primary tissue. HPV-positive calls (Kraken2 ≥10 papillomavirus reads) among `r comma(stats$n_screened)` human RNA-seq libraries from the unbiased skin-and-related-tissue sweep, stratified by sample provenance. Positivity percentages are shown with Wilson 95% confidence intervals; odds ratios (95% CI) and *P* values are from a logistic regression with clinical primary tissue as the reference class (overall likelihood-ratio *P* = `r fp(stats$lr_p)`). Cell-line and engineered libraries carry roughly four- to five-fold higher odds of HPV positivity than primary tissue, and their positive calls are predominantly the integrated mucosal types HPV16 and HPV18, the expected signature of cell-line contamination. {#tbl-positivity}

### Skin neoplasia carries only trace HPV, not productive infection, and this is not a depth artefact

The central question, whether HPV is transcriptionally active in cSCC/AK or present only as a hit-and-run footprint, was resolved by the depth-normalised burden analysis (@fig-rpm). Productive controls (warts/condyloma) were detected at or above the control-calibrated detection floor (`r p1(stats$floor_rpm)` RPM) in `r pp(stats$ctrl_pctfloor)`% of libraries, with a median burden of `r comma(stats$ctrl_medrpm)` RPM. In stark contrast, the neoplasia tier reached this floor in only `r pp(stats$neo_pctfloor)`% of libraries (median `r formatC(stats$neo_medrpm, format="f", digits=3)` RPM), close to unselected skin (`r pp(stats$uns_pctfloor)`%) and far below the productive controls, despite being the most deeply sequenced tier of all (median `r mreads(stats$neo_medreads)` vs `r mreads(stats$ctrl_medreads)` reads for controls).

![Depth-normalised HPV transcript burden distinguishes productive warts and EV from a trace-level signal in skin neoplasia and unselected skin. Each point is one Kraken2-screened RNA-seq library, scored as papillomavirus reads per million (RPM: Papillomaviridae clade reads ÷ (unclassified + root reads) × 10⁶) and grouped by population tier: unselected skin (*n* = `r comma(stats$uns_n)`), cell lines (`r comma(stats$cl2_n)`), productive controls (warts/condyloma; `r stats$ctrl_n`), cutaneous neoplasia (cSCC/actinic keratosis; `r stats$neo_n`), and other tissues (`r comma(stats$oth_n)`). Points are jittered horizontally; boxes show the median and interquartile range; the *y*-axis is log~10~-scaled (RPM + 0.05). The dashed red line marks the control-calibrated detection floor (`r p1(stats$floor_rpm)` RPM, the 5th percentile of productive-control libraries). Productive controls lie far above the floor (median `r comma(stats$ctrl_medrpm)` RPM; `r pp(stats$ctrl_pctfloor)`% of libraries above floor), whereas neoplasia and unselected skin sit near zero (`r pp(stats$neo_pctfloor)`% and `r pp(stats$uns_pctfloor)`% above floor) despite neoplasia being the most deeply sequenced tier. Epidermodysplasia verruciformis (EV) libraries were metadata-flagged as cultures and fall within the cell-line tier.](figure1.png){#fig-rpm}

This gap is not explained by sequencing depth. Modelling productive-level detection (RPM ≥ floor) as a function of population tier and log sequencing depth, neoplasia did not significantly differ from unselected skin after depth adjustment (OR `r fo(stats$m2b_or)`, 95% CI `r oci(stats$m2b_lo, stats$m2b_hi)`, *P* = `r fp(stats$m2b_p)`), whereas productive controls exceeded unselected skin by three orders of magnitude (Fisher exact OR `r fo(stats$fish_or)`, 95% CI `r oci(stats$fish_lo, stats$fish_hi)`, *P* = `r fp(stats$fish_p)`). Sequencing depth did raise the odds of detecting *any* HPV read (OR per log₁₀ reads `r fo(stats$m2a_l10_or)`, `r oci(stats$m2a_l10_lo, stats$m2a_l10_hi)`, *P* = `r fp(stats$m2a_l10_p)`), but within the neoplasia tier alone deeper sequencing bought no additional productive-level detection (OR `r fo(stats$neo_depth_or)`, 95% CI `r oci(stats$neo_depth_lo, stats$neo_depth_hi)`, *P* = `r fp(stats$neo_depth_p)`). Even the deepest cSCC/AK libraries did not cross the productive floor. Consistent with a residual hit-and-run footprint rather than active infection, neoplasia showed a modest excess of *trace* HPV (any read; OR `r fo(stats$m2a_or)`, 95% CI `r oci(stats$m2a_lo, stats$m2a_hi)`, *P* = `r fp(stats$m2a_p)` vs unselected skin), but this trace signal never amounted to productive burden. The depth-matched detection profile confirms the same pattern non-parametrically: across every sequencing-depth bin, productive controls detect at 90–100% while neoplasia and unselected skin stay near zero (@suppfig-depth). At population scale, then, HPV in cutaneous neoplasia is a low-level, transcriptionally inactive trace, the signature predicted by the hit-and-run model, and not the productive infection seen in warts.

### The assay detects bona fide productive cutaneous HPV, including genuine infections in primary skin

That the trace neoplasia signal reflects biology rather than assay insensitivity is established by the positive controls, which the pipeline detects robustly. Among targeted wart/EV libraries, `r round(stats$m3_ctrl_pct)`% (`r stats$m3_ctrl_prod`/`r stats$m3_ctrl_n`) met the strict productive criterion (≥3 L1 major-capsid reads), spanning the classic cutaneous repertoire: wart-associated alpha types (HPV2, HPV27, HPV57), low-risk mucocutaneous types (HPV6, HPV11), and beta/gamma types (@supptbl-types). Productive infection was significantly more frequent in these controls than among HPV-positive neoplasia libraries (`r round(stats$m3_neo_pct)`%, `r stats$m3_neo_prod`/`r stats$m3_neo_n`; Fisher exact OR `r fo(stats$m3_or)`, 95% CI `r oci(stats$m3_lo, stats$m3_hi)`, *P* = `r fp(stats$m3_p)`).

Even so, a minority of genuine, non-cell-line skin lesions did harbour unambiguous productive infection, showing that the trace-level population result does not exclude real cutaneous HPV disease. The strongest cases were a cSCC/CIN skin library productively infected with the gamma-type HPV12 (99.8% genome coverage; L1 = 1,803, L2 = 3,546 reads), two HPV16-productive cSCC libraries, and two "other-tissue" libraries (an actinic keratosis and a carcinoma) co-infected with the gamma types HPV96 and HPV182 at near-complete genome coverage (98–99%) and high depth (60–245×), with hundreds of L1 and L2 reads each (@supptbl-productive). These represent the answer to the non-traditional-tissue and productive-infection questions: bona fide, high-confidence productive beta/gamma-HPV infections do occur in primary skin and skin-associated lesions, but they are the exception against a null background.

### The HPV type landscape is partitioned by population

The HPV-type-by-tier catalogue (@supptbl-types) recapitulates these conclusions in a single descriptive view: the high-risk mucosal types HPV16/HPV18 are confined almost entirely to cell-line and engineered material; productive controls are dominated by cutaneous wart and low-risk types (HPV57, HPV2, HPV27, HPV6/11, plus diverse beta/gamma types); unselected skin shows a sparse, low-burden scatter of beta/gamma types; and the genuine neoplasia-associated productive infections are the gamma/alpha types noted above (HPV12, HPV16, HPV96, HPV182). The epidemiological reservoir of cutaneous beta/gamma HPV in healthy skin is therefore real but transcriptionally quiet, productive disease is the property of warts/EV, and HPV-associated cutaneous neoplasia carries the virus, when at all, as a trace, non-productive footprint.

### A Bayesian re-analysis under weakly-informative priors confirms the contrasts

To confirm these conclusions are not artefacts of the frequentist framework or of the near-complete separation in the strongest contrast, we refitted the positivity, depth-controlled detection and productivity models as Bayesian logistic regressions under weakly-informative priors (Normal(0, 2.5) on the log-odds coefficients; Methods), summarising each effect by its posterior median odds ratio, 95% credible interval (CrI) and posterior probability of direction (@supptbl-bayes). The contamination enrichment and the trace excess were reproduced essentially unchanged and with near-certainty (cell-line OR `r bor(bget("M1_cell_line"))`, engineered OR `r bor(bget("M1_engineered"))`, neoplasia trace OR `r bor(bget("M2a_neoplasia_vs_skin_any"))`; each with P(OR > 1) > 0.999). Crucially, the productive-controls-versus-skin contrast, whose frequentist odds ratio (`r fo(stats$fish_or)`) is inflated and unstable under separation, regularised to a finite but still enormous posterior OR of `r bor(bget("M2c_controls_vs_skin_floor"))` (`r bor(bget("M2c_controls_vs_skin_floor_studentt"))` under a heavier-tailed Student-*t* prior): the productive gap between warts/EV and skin is real, not a separation artefact. Deeper sequencing within neoplasia again bought no productive-level detection (OR `r bor(bget("M2d_depth_within_neoplasia"))`), and the warts/EV-versus-neoplasia productivity contrast held (OR `r bor(bget("M3_controls_vs_neoplasia"))`, P(OR > 1) = `r pdir(bget("M3_controls_vs_neoplasia")$p_gt1)`). The only effect the posterior nuances is the depth-adjusted neoplasia-versus-skin comparison at productive level, whose credible interval includes no difference (OR `r bor(bget("M2b_neoplasia_vs_skin_floor"))`, consistent with the frequentist *P* = `r fp(stats$m2b_p)`) but which places `r pdir(bget("M2b_neoplasia_vs_skin_floor")$p_gt1)` posterior probability on a *small* elevation; even if genuine, such a footprint is negligible beside the three-orders-of-magnitude productive-control effect and does not alter the trace-not-productive conclusion.
